Personal Philosophy

JACK RAU
Mechanical Engineering  ·  UConn '28

About Me

I'm Jack Rau, a mechanical engineering student at UConn from Ridgewood, NJ. I came here to build things — not metaphorically, but literally. I want hands-on engineering work, the kind where you can point at something and say: that works because I made it work.

I'm competitive by nature. I play football, I follow tech closely, and I spend a lot of time thinking about how things are designed — why some function beautifully and others fall apart. I think caring about craft matters, whether you're writing an essay, mapping invasive species in your hometown, or building a composting system that actually has to perform.

I believe in committing fully to what you take on. If something is worth doing, it deserves real investment — that's what separates work you're proud of from work you forget. Passion gives any project its meaning, and I'd rather go all-in on a few things than spread myself thin across many.

Plans change, and I'm genuinely okay with that. I don't need everything figured out in advance. The uncertainty of what comes next is part of what makes each project worth starting.

Studio Portfolio  ·  Curation Assignment

Making things
in response to
the world

Three artifacts. One pattern: encountering a problem and making something in response — an argument, a map, an object.

Curator's Note

At first glance, an essay on identity, a map of invasive trees, and a compost bin don't seem to belong together. But they share something essential: each one starts with a problem in the world and asks what I can actually do about it.

In my philosophy essay, the problem is invisible — the quiet, ongoing pressure that environments and platforms exert on who we are. My response was to think carefully and write honestly, using language to work through something I couldn't fully see. In the Tree of Heaven project, the problem was visible and urgent — a plant taking over a landscape — and my response was to go outside, collect real data, draw a map, and hand it to someone with the power to act. With the compost bin, the problem was mechanical: a natural process producing a harmful byproduct. My response was to design and build a solution from scratch.

What connects these three artifacts is a pattern I'm starting to recognize in myself: I don't just observe problems, I make things in response to them. Sometimes that means an argument, sometimes a visualization, sometimes a physical object. Each mode taught me something different about what I'm capable of — and together, they show the kind of maker and thinker I'm becoming.

Curator's note: 209 words  ·  Target: 200–250

Selected Works  ·  Three Artifacts

Artifact 01  ·  Written Essay

WHAT MAKES YOU YOU

2023 Written Essay Senior Year Philosophy & Literature ~3 Pages

A three-page philosophical and literary essay written for a senior-year Philosophy and Literature course, this piece explores the forces that shape individual identity — examining how environment, social media, and cultural pressures construct, alter, and sometimes destabilize the self. Drawing on personal insight rather than a single prescribed framework, the essay presents an original argument about the nature of selfhood. Composed and revised using a word processor over several weeks of seminar discussion, it showcases skills in critical thinking, analytical writing, and synthesizing complex ideas into a clear, personal intellectual voice.

Modes of meaning

Linguistic

Artifact 02  ·  Research & Community Action

MAPPING THE INVASION

2024 Data Map & Report Group Project AP Environmental Science

Created for AP Environmental Science, this collaborative community action project documents the spread of Ailanthus altissima — the invasive Tree of Heaven — throughout a local town. Working in a small group, I was responsible for the annotated town map: marking high-density zones and population estimates using iPhone photography and Google Maps. The team physically removed trees and submitted our findings and photos to town officials to raise awareness. This project demonstrates data collection, visual communication, environmental literacy, and civic engagement across linguistic, visual, and spatial modes.

Modes of meaning

LinguisticVisualSpatial

Artifact 03  ·  Engineered Object

SOLAR SMART COMPOST BIN

2023 Physical Build Self-Designed Applied Engineering Club

Designed and built during junior year through the Applied Engineering club, this solar-powered composting system addresses methane production in traditional compost bins by using solar panels to drive fans that actively circulate oxygen through the chamber — preventing harmful gas buildup. Constructed from metal, wood, specialty sealants, adhesives, and paint, all selected and assembled independently, the bin was fully functional and placed into active school use. The design was original. This artifact demonstrates systems thinking, structural design, sustainable engineering, hands-on fabrication, and problem-solving through making.

Modes of meaning

SpatialGestural
